title: LoopLedger: Tracing Observe-Think-Act Cycles in Offline Agents
abstract: This paper argues that goal-directed behavior comes from explicit control loops and traceable state transitions rather than longer prompts alone.
findings: Explicit control loops make behavior inspectable and easier to debug than prompt-only baselines.
limitations: Focuses on offline tasks rather than live environments.

title: Goal Contracts for Bounded Literature Review Assistants
abstract: Structured goals, success criteria, and stop rules are treated as control inputs rather than prose decoration.
findings: Explicit success and stop criteria change downstream planning and evaluation behavior.

title: StateLens: Separating Context, State, and Outcomes in Multi-Step Assistants
abstract: The paper distinguishes transient context from durable external state and shows how traces expose causal state changes.
findings: Separating context from external state makes multi-step failures easier to diagnose.

title: MemoryPolicy: When Notes Help and When They Mislead
abstract: Stored notes improve continuity only when retrieval and forgetting policies are explicit. Stale notes create confident but brittle synthesis.
findings: Memory must include retrieval and forgetting policy, not just storage.

title: Interface Contracts and Evidence Linking for Local Research Tools
abstract: Research tools help only when tool outputs remain linked to the claims they support through explicit evidence-linking contracts.
findings: Tool interfaces change both capability and what evidence can ground the final review.

title: PlanSketch: Lightweight Decomposition for Small Review Tasks
abstract: Small literature reviews benefit from lightweight decomposition and selective replanning instead of exhaustive search trees.
findings: Lightweight decomposition adds enough structure for small review tasks without heavy formal planning.

title: ReflectCheck: Reflection Improves Reviews Only Under Evidence-Backed Verification
abstract: Self-critique improves review quality only when explicit verification signals force corrective action rather than generic introspection.
findings: Reflection helps only when tied to evidence-backed verification and changed behavior.

title: BoundedStop: Clarification and Handoff as Correct Agent Outcomes
abstract: The paper frames clarification, stop, and handoff as correctness conditions in bounded autonomy rather than operational afterthoughts.
findings: Clarification and handoff are valid bounded outcomes when evidence or scope blocks safe completion.

title: BareModel Baselines for Review Writing
abstract: Prompt-only review writing is fluent but under-grounded because it cannot inspect a corpus or verify claims against local evidence. The paper uses scripted baselines as the closest control.
findings: Model-only baselines motivate architecture because fluency does not imply grounded success.

title: Tool-Rich, Memoryless Review Systems
abstract: Fresh tool access helps a review agent find new papers, but weak retention causes earlier evidence to fall out of working context.
findings: Tool-rich memoryless systems find fresh evidence but often lose earlier support during synthesis.

title: Memory-Rich, Tool-Poor Research Assistants
abstract: Stored notes improve continuity, but without enough search capability the agent cannot recover from gaps in its memorized evidence.
findings: Memory-rich tool-poor systems preserve continuity but struggle when they need fresh evidence outside stored notes.

title: Comparing Review Architectures by Task Shape
abstract: Architecture choice should be defended by observed outcomes on bounded tasks rather than by slogans such as 'agents are always better'. The paper explicitly contrasts model-only and scripted baselines before moving to richer agent variants.
findings: Task-architecture fit should be justified with observed outcomes and explicit failure diagnoses.

title: Stale Reflection Notes and the Cost of Unchecked Recall
abstract: Legacy notes tend to overstate reflection benefits unless they are revalidated against current local evidence.
findings: Unchecked stale memory makes reflection look more reliable than the evidence supports.

title: CoverageTrap: Why Greedy Paper Selection Misses Evidence Linking
abstract: Greedy top-k selection misses papers about evidence linking and verifiable claims when relevant work uses different surface terms.
findings: Coverage-aware selection and topic-specific replanning recover missing evidence better than top-k greedy selection.
